text: This is the case of a sixty three-year-old male with pancreatic adenocarcinoma. A CT scan demonstrates an area of five centimeters where the tumor is adherent to the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon isolates the superior mesenteric vein and clears the surrounding area of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The pancreatic head and duodenum are retracted anteriorly and to the left to facilitate proximal SMA dissection. Electrocautery is used for posterior SMA dissection, carefully removing the perineural sheath from the vessel to optimize oncologic margins. The pancreaticoduodenal artery is dissected, ligated, and transected. Periportal lymph nodes are dissected, mobilizing the tissue with the specimen.

The specimen is retracted inferiorly as the surgeon begins isolating the portal vein, placing a vessel loop around it. A graft for venous reconstruction is created from the left renal vein, with its insertion into the inferior vena cava divided using a stapler. The distal stump is transected sharply and oversewn with running VivoProLean suture. The left gonadal vein is preserved for kidney venous drainage. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified, ligated, and transected, followed by the ligation and transection of the splenic vein.

The surgeon performs the final dissection of the anterior attachments to the superior mesenteric artery, leaving the specimen attached only by the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein. The left gastric vein is visibly preserved to ensure venous drainage of the stomach. A clamp is placed on the superior mesenteric vein, followed by a clamp on the portal vein, which is then transected.

The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen from its final attachment to the superior mesenteric vein and places it in the left upper quadrant. Immediate reconstruction begins with the positioning of the renal vein graft and the start of proximal anastomosis using a running 5-0 Prolene suture. The superior mesenteric artery is clamped to minimize edema during reconstruction. The distal anastomosis between the portal vein and renal graft is completed with a running Prolene suture. An intraoperative ultrasound is used to verify the patency and flow of the graft.
The surgeon removes the clamps in sequence, starting with the distal clamp, followed by the proximal clamp, and finally the superior mesenteric artery clamp, after completing the anastomosis.
